flashcards.front: What is a static data member in C++?
flashcards.back: A static data member is a class member that is shared among all objects of the class. There is only one copy of the static member regardless of how many objects are created.

flashcards.front: Where is a static data member declared?
flashcards.back: Static data members are declared inside the class body (usually in the private or public section) but defined outside the class definition.

flashcards.front: What is the syntax to access a static data member?
flashcards.back: Static members can be accessed using the class name and scope resolution operator (ClassName::member) or through object name (object.member).

flashcards.front: What is a static member function?
flashcards.back: A static member function is a function that belongs to the class rather than any object. It can be called without creating any object of the class.

flashcards.front: Can static member functions access 'this' pointer?
flashcards.back: No, static member functions do not have a 'this' pointer because they are not associated with any particular object.

flashcards.front: What is the key difference between static and instance members?
flashcards.back: Instance members belong to individual objects and each object has its own copy. Static members are shared by all objects and exist as a single copy for the entire class.

flashcards.front: How do you initialize a static data member?
flashcards.back: Static data members must be defined once outside the class using the syntax: datatype ClassName::memberName = value;

flashcards.front: Can static member functions be const?
flashcards.back: No, static member functions cannot be const because they do not operate on any object and cannot access 'this' pointer.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of static member functions?
flashcards.back: Static member functions are used to access or modify static data members and can be called without creating any object, often used for utility functions or factory methods.

flashcards.front: Where should static data members typically be initialized?
flashcards.back: Static data members should be initialized in a source file (.cpp) outside any function, typically at global scope.

flashcards.front: What is a static local variable?
flashcards.back: A static local variable is a variable that retains its value between function calls. It is initialized only once when the function is first called.

flashcards.front: Can regular member functions access static members?
flashcards.back: Yes, regular member functions can access both static data members and static member functions directly without any special syntax.

flashcards.front: What happens if a static data member is not explicitly defined outside the class?
flashcards.back: It results in a linker error (undefined reference) because the compiler only declares the static member but does not allocate memory for it.

flashcards.front: What is a static const member in C++?
flashcards.back: A static const member is a constant that belongs to the class. In C++11, it can be initialized directly in the class body if it is an integral or enumeration type.
Static members are used to share data across all objects, maintain global-like variables in a class context, implement counters, factory patterns, and singleton design patterns.
